There are currently no approved vaccines for human parainfluenza viruses (HPIV), nor are there any late-stage (Phase 3) trials nearing completion or regulatory review anywhere in the world. As of 2025, experimental vaccines (like Moderna's mRNA-1653 or various live-attenuated candidates) remain in early to mid-stage clinical trials (Phase 1 or 2). Given that vaccine approval typically requires successful Phase 3 trials and months of regulatory review, it is functionally impossible for a new vaccine to be approved and added to the Wikipedia list within the 1-3 month timeframe. The probabilities start at the minimum allowed bounds and only slightly account for the increasing base uncertainty over time (or Wikipedia vandalism/errors).

Human bocavirus (HBoV) is an emerging pathogen whose exact pathogenic role is still debated due to high coinfection rates. Extensive searches yielded no evidence of any HBoV vaccine in clinical trials, let alone approaching regulatory approval. A 2023 report explicitly states 'there is no vaccine in development for human use', and searching clinicaltrials.gov for 'bocavirus vaccine' returns 0 results. Given the knowledge cutoff is October 2025, it is impossible for a vaccine to go from pre-clinical (or non-existent) to approved and listed on Wikipedia by January 2026. Because of the extremely rigid, multi-year timeline of vaccine development, the probability of a vaccine being successfully developed and Wikipedia being updated to 'Yes' in the next 1 to 3 months is effectively zero aside from the possibility of Wikipedia vandalism on the resolution dates. Therefore, probabilities reflect the lowest allowable bounds, increasing slightly for more distant horizons to account for marginally more uncertainty.

Lilly King set the 100m long course breaststroke world record of 1:04.13 in 2017, and it has stood for 8 years. I verified that this record was not broken at the 2025 World Aquatics Championships in Singapore, where Anna Elendt won gold with a time of 1:05.19 (a full second slower). Since the rest of the year (late autumn and winter) primarily consists of short course (25m) competitions, there are very few opportunities for long course world records to be broken between October 2025 and January 2026. Therefore, the probability that the record will hold is exceedingly high, only decreasing slightly for the later resolution dates to reflect the marginally longer time horizon.

Ariarne Titmus holds the 200m freestyle long course world record at 1:52.23, an exceptionally fast time set in June 2024. As of her recent retirement in October 2025, this record still currently stands. The 2025 World Aquatics Championships already concluded in July/August, meaning there are no global championship long-course events scheduled between the cutoff date (October 26, 2025) and the final resolution date (January 24, 2026). The international swimming calendar in late fall and early winter is largely dedicated to short course competitions or early-season, un-tapered long course meets (e.g., the U.S. Open in early December). Swimmers rarely break highly competitive long course individual world records mid-season, as that requires peak physical tapering typically reserved for national trials or summer global championships. The only active swimmer within striking distance is Mollie O'Callaghan (PB 1:52.48), but the lack of peak long-course events over the next three months makes a new record highly implausible. The small residual uncertainty primarily accounts for unforeseen anomalous mid-season performances or highly unlikely retroactive disqualifications.

The question asks if Vaishali R's official Elo rating (according to the Wikipedia FIDE Rankings page) will be functionally 1% higher on Nov 2, Nov 25, and Jan 24 than on Oct 26. A 1% increase over her known baseline of 2473 amounts to nearly +25 rating points (requiring a rating of >= 2498). For the first two resolution dates, the November 2025 rating list will apply. Since the cut-off for the Nov list is late October, she would have needed a massive +25 performance purely within the month of October. My searches did not reveal any active October classical tournaments for her, making a +25 gain basically nil for Nov 2 and Nov 25. For the January 2026 date, she has an additional two months of play to hit 2498. While mathematically possible given her high volatility (historically gaining/losing 20+ points in a single month at events like the Grand Swiss), it is still statistically unlikely. Therefore, uncertainty increases slightly over the longer time horizon but is still heavily weighted towards NO.

The Wikipedia page 'List of infectious diseases' currently lists the vaccine status for Ascariasis as 'No'. Since creating, testing, approving, and recognizing a new vaccine takes years or decades, there is no realistic chance that one will be developed and added to the list in the next 1-3 months. Any non-zero chance comes purely from edge cases like Wikipedia vandalism or editing errors remaining uncorrected on the specific resolution dates. Respecting the bounds of 0.05–0.95 and increasing uncertainty for further dates, I output 0.05, 0.06, and 0.07.

The Wikipedia page for 'List of infectious diseases' currently says 'No' for an Enterovirus vaccine. While vaccines for Enterovirus A71 (a cause of Hand, Foot, and Mouth disease) have existed in China and elsewhere for years, Wikipedia's table has not been updated, likely because "Enterovirus infection" is treated as a generic category (for which there is no generic vaccine), or because the FDA/EMA has not formally approved one, or simply due to editor neglect. There is no news of upcoming FDA/EMA approvals or impending global rollouts between Oct 2025 and Jan 2026 that would serve as a natural catalyst for an edit. The only probable cause for a change from 'No' to 'Yes' in the short term would be a spontaneous edit by a reader (possibly someone from the forecasting community realizing a technical inaccuracy given EV-71 vaccines). Given the strong norms against forecast-driven editing and the likelihood that generic "Enterovirus infection" stays as "No" to distinguish it from specific polio/HFMD entries, the probability remains very low. The uncertainty increases slightly over time to account for the baseline rate of Wikipedia table overhauls or spontaneous, unprompted corrections.

The Wikipedia page currently lists 'No' for a Pneumonia vaccine. Because pneumonia is a syndrome caused by many different pathogens rather than a single specific infectious agent, a universal 'pneumonia vaccine' does not exist (though vaccines for specific causes, like pneumococcus, do). Wikipedia editors are generally strict about these medical distinctions, making an organic change to 'Yes' highly unlikely over the next 1 to 3 months. Any change to 'Yes' would likely be the result of either a misguided edit (confusing the pneumococcal vaccine for a general pneumonia vaccine) or intentional vandalism (e.g., market manipulation), both of which are typically reverted quickly. The baseline risk of encountering a temporary, un-reverted 'Yes' edit on the exact resolution days is low but slightly increases over the forecast horizon.

Rickettsialpox is a mild and self-limited disease caused by Rickettsia akari. It is easily treated with antibiotics like doxycycline. There is no existing vaccine and no active vaccine development program, as confirmed by current literature and CDC statements (which explicitly state there are no vaccines for any rickettsial diseases). Because a vaccine requires years of clinical trials and regulatory approval, the probability of one being legitimately developed and approved in the next few months is functionally zero. The only risk of the Wikipedia page claiming 'Yes' on the resolution dates comes from vandalism or a gross editing error, which is highly unlikely to occur and persist on the specific resolution dates. Thus, the probability remains at the lowest allowed limit (with a strictly increasing minimal uncertainty over time).

The baseline rating on 2025-10-26 is highly likely to be Caruana's official October FIDE rating of 2789. A 1% increase equates to a target rating of 2816.89 (so effectively 2817+). Even if Wikipedia updates early with his post-tournament live rating, it would be around 2800, thus requiring 2828+.  

Caruana had a successful performance at the US Championship in October 2025, reclaiming his 4th straight title. Live rating trackers (such as 2700chess) showed his live rating hovering just under 2800 (around 2799) following his results (including draws to lower-rated players like Wesley So). This ~2799 rating will become his official November 2025 rating, falling well short of the 2817 target. 

For the 2025-11-02 and 2025-11-25 resolution dates, Wikipedia will reflect his October or November official ratings (neither of which is 2817+), giving this a virtually zero probability. For the 2026-01-24 date, Caruana would need to add at least 17-20 Elo points in November and December to reach 2817. Gaining that much rating at the 2800 level requires an historic string of performances (equivalent to a 3000+ TPR over multiple events without any setbacks). Though Caruana is uniquely talented, expecting an all-time record streak in a specific two-month window carries an extremely low base rate. I assign near-floor bounds, with slight scaling for the extended time horizon.
